data.title: AVK: Preserves Investor Capital And Maintains Consistent Dividends (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

As market indexes hover near their all-time highs, it can be scary to accumulate equities at this time. However, there are many attractive opportunities across income funds as valuations remain suppressed due to higher interest rates. Advent Convertible & Income Fund (AVK) takes a hybrid approach by utilizing convertible securities that can both generate income and participate in market rallies. When I previously covered AVK, I issued a hold rating due to the high use of leverage that limits growth potential. However, the Fed has initiated another interest rate cut since then, so I wanted to revisit the fund's performance and value proposition as we approach the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that AVK's share price has increased by 0.5%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to partially participate in the positive momentum of the market this year. When including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return jumps up to nearly 13.4% over the same time frame. AVK now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.1% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. The fund has maintained a long streak of consistent payouts, which makes AVK a great option for investors seeking income stability.

Additionally, the fund now trades at a favorable discount to NAV valuation. I believe that the fund is likely to see growth in a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months. As interest rates continue to decline, the negative impact of leverage will likely be reduced as well. I believe that AVK has continued to demonstrate its resilience during periods of uncertainty, which also makes it a great option for investors that simply want to buy and hold over a long time horizon. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that AVK implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, AVK has total managed assets of $928.7M that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund has a primary goal of providing favorable total returns while prioritizing a high current income. In order to achieve this goal, AVK allocates its assets across a diverse range of income producing securities. However, AVK puts a specific focus on convertible securities, which act as a hybrid instrument. Convertible securities are structured as bonds that produce income, but can be converted into common shares if management believes there is growth potential. The fund's breakdown by security allocation is as follows:

  • Convertible Securities: 48.73%
  • High-Yield Securities: 40.96%
  • Equities: 7.33%
  • Cash: 1.79%
  • Collateralized Loan Obligation: 1.19%

Expanding on this diversity, AVK allocates its assets across a diverse range of sectors on a global scale. Approximately 79.8% of its assets are allocated to US securities, while the remaining 20.2% is allocated to international securities. Additionally, we can see below that AVK is most heavily allocated to Technology investments at 19.32% of the portfolio. This is followed by an allocation to Financials and Industrial investments, which account for 17.27% and 14.20% of the portfolio, respectively.

One of the most attractive parts of the fund is that AVK maintains exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world. Looking at the holdings below, we can see that AVK's top position is in the Advent Convertible Bond ETF (ACVT). This is followed by exposure to many other notable names, including Boeing (BA), Coinbase (COIN), Uber Technologies (UBER), Alibaba (BABA), and Snowflake (SNOW), just to name a few. However, we must remember that AVK maintains exposure to these positions through income instruments, so the growth potential may be limited during bull markets.

Since AVK is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, AVK now trades at a discount to NAV of 4.61%, which is slightly larger than the discount at the time of my last coverage. For reference, AVK has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.33% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the chart below, we can see that the fund trades at a reasonable price-to-NAV valuation compared to its historical range. I believe that long-term investors may be able to take advantage of this price disconnect and would be rewarded for accumulating shares here.

Performance Insights & Risk

Although the fund has the ability to convert its assets into common shares, I believe that investors should limit their expectations around AVK's ability to provide a meaningful level of price growth. To demonstrate this, we can refer to the ten-year performance chart below. As we can see, AVK's share price has declined by 4.71% over the last decade, which indicates that the fund has failed to participate in the bull markets over the last ten years. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 176.2% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

Despite the limited price growth, I believe that the fund has demonstrated its ability to preserve investor capital over time. This is ideal for retirees that want to collect steady income at a high yield, without the risk of seeing their invested capital erode over time. Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that AVK only makes sense for investors seeking income generation, rather than a high total return from the market.

As part of the fund's operating strategy, AVK utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 37.68% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $350M. I consider this an aggressive use of leverage that can potentially threaten the fund's growth in this high-interest rate environment. Investors need to consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated. Since interest rates are high, the spread between AVK's cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities may be thinner.

One of the main challenges that can threaten AVK's success is the large allocation to securities that have lower credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below BBB are classified as below investment grade and are considered to be riskier. Borrowers with a low credit rating have historically been more vulnerable to the risk of defaults. These are usually borrowers with less consistent cash flows or that may be more reliant on debt financing to support operations. The fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 12.32%
  • Below Investment Grade: (BB - D): 46.33%
  • Not Rated: 41.36%

Due to the combination between the fund's use of leverage and the allocation to below investment grade securities, these contribute to the fund's vulnerability to shifts in the interest rate environment. As we can see below, AVK's share price has shared an inverse relationship to interest rates in the last five years. When rates were cut to near zero levels during the pandemic, this improved operating spreads, and AVK's share price rapidly increased throughout 2020 and 2021. Conversely, AVK's share price has remained suppressed since rates were aggressively increased throughout 2022 and 2023.

The Fed has initiated its third 25 basis point cut to interest rates for 2025. As interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, this should improve AVK's performance and allow the fund to experience better price growth. Although rates are still relatively high, the fund's risk profile should continue to decline since defaults will become less likely. Additionally, lower interest rates may encourage more businesses to seek debt financing, which means that AVK may be able to capitalize on a great level of new investment activity to grow earnings in the future. However, there's always the risk that additional interest rate cuts may happen slower than anticipated.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1172 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.1%. One of the strongest aspects of the fund is that AVK has maintained a long streak of consistent payouts through the changing market cycles. Therefore, AVK continues to be a great option for investors that want to buy-and-hold a high quality fund that will not lower payouts over time. However, the fund doesn't have the best track record of providing dividend raises over time.

Despite this, I believe that the consistent payouts should be commended because they provide investors with the opportunity to create their own growth. This can be achieved by reinvesting portions of all distributions back into accumulating more shares or by adding new capital on a consistent basis. It's also reassuring that AVK was able to maintain consistent payouts through the pandemic, shifting interest rates, and even periods of elevated inflation. This consistency really emphasizes that management cares about dividend sustainability. Therefore, I do not anticipate any cuts to the dividend any time soon.

Lastly, it's worth mentioning that the distributions received from AVK have the potential to offer some tax efficiency. For instance, the latest available secton19(a) notice indicates that 29.1% of the payout was funded by net investment income, while the remaining 70.9% of the distribution is classified as return of capital. Although net investment income has the highest tax burden for investors, return of capital payouts tend to be more tax-friendly.

This is because return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and, therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, return of capital distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. Therefore, this gives AVK some flexibility to be utilized outside of tax-advantaged accounts.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on AVK to a buy at this time. The fund trades at an attractive valuation, and the portfolio continues to show its efficiency by preserving investor capital over time. The fund has maintained a long track record of consistent payouts, and this makes it a great option for buy-and-hold investors that want to prioritize a reliable stream of potentially tax-efficient income. As interest rates continue to trend lower, I believe that operating spread will improve and lead to earnings growth over time.

data.title: Viomi Technology: Available At A Good Entry Point
data.description:
Viomi Technology (VIOT), a provider of better drinking water solutions, is still way up with 2025 coming to a close, but it has lost a big chunk of its YTD gains after gradually moving lower in the past four months or so. However, while the decline in the stock is discouraging to see, there are several reasons why there is a case to be made in favor of stepping in at this time and being long VIOT, in spite of the recent struggles in the stock.

VIOT is in the midst of a major retreat

A previous article from March 2025 written by myself mentioned how the stock came close to finally overcoming the resistance around the $2.00 price point in the charts, but in the end, VIOT was unable to get it done as the stock fell back below the hurdle that had blocked the way higher for some time. On the other hand, support also seemed to be present, which provided VIOT with some measure of protection against extended declines and a solid base from which to make renewed attempts to break through and move higher. In the end, I chose to rate VIOT a buy in the article.

The chart above shows how having faith in a future breakout proved to be justified because VIOT succeeded in breaking out in July, which seems to have been enabled by massive buying into the stock that month. The ensuing rally brought VIOT to a 52-week high of $4.33 per ADS in August, but it is worth noting how the stock has steadily moved lower in the months that followed.

The stock closed at $2.32 on December 11, which means VIOT has lost 46.4% since the August high. On the other hand, the decline has brought the stock back to the $2.00-2.40 price region, or the same region it struggled to get by in March, as shown in the chart. The stock topped out at $2.31 in March, for instance, which is just one cent below $2.32, or where it last closed. This could help enable a rebound in the stock since what used to be resistance in the charts tends to become support. If the $2.00-2.40 region was hard to get by on the way higher, then it is likely to also be hard to get by on the way lower. If support is where resistance used to be, then a rebound in the stock could be close by.

Furthermore, it is worth pointing out how in the chart above, the stock rallied from a low of $0.50 to a high of $4.33, and if we assume VIOT has been retracing this move upwards with the move downwards of the past several months, then it is interesting to note that the 50% Fibonacci retracement of $0.50 to $4.33 is $2.41, just as the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $0.50 to $4.33 is $1.96.

This matches well with the $2.00-2.40 region identified as somewhere VIOT might be able to find enough support to put together a rebound in the stock and a move higher. If all this holds, including the existence of support in or around the $2.00-2.40 region, the recent slide in the stock of the past several months may be close to over. The decline of the past four months or so is also long in the tooth, which by itself could enable some sort of a bounce.

Valuations warrant taking a shot at long VIOT

Potential support close by is not the only reason to consider long VIOT. For instance, the large reduction in the price of the stock has brought valuations down to the point VIOT is now valued at close to the net cash on the balance sheet, which is likely to appeal to those interested in stocks trading at what could be a bargain.

VIOT only releases earnings reports twice a year, but the balance sheet of the most recent, or H1 FY2025 report shows cash and cash equivalents of $99M, short-term deposits of $52.6M, and short-term investments of $15.08M. The sum of all these adds up to $166.68M, which is more than the current market cap of $158M.

VIOT does have $18.8M of debt to account for, but it still means VIOT is getting close to being valued at the net cash, or what can be converted into cash, on the books. A further decline in the price of the stock would push VIOT towards having the distinction of being valued at less than the net cash on the books. This is likely to draw potential buyers, and this could help support a rebound in the stock, especially in combination with support in the charts pointed out earlier.

Why VIOT could be seen as undervalued at current valuations

Other multiples for VIOT are similarly appealing, especially after the recent cut in the stock price. For instance, VIOT has a book value of $218.61M with total assets of $410.63M and total liabilities of $192.03M, which converts to a book value of $3.22 per ADS since the total number of ADS/ADRs is 67,963K. VIOT is thus available at less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.72x and the stock at $2.32 per ADS. An argument can be made that VIOT is undervalued based on the state of the balance sheet.

VIOT looks undervalued from another angle. In the latest earnings report, revenue grew by 76.6% YoY to RMB1,477.6M, or $206.3M, in H1 FY2025, which resulted in a 64.7% increase in net income to RMB120.4M, or $16.8M. The bottom line did not increase as fast as the top line due to changes in product mix, which included lower contributions during the reporting period from higher-margin products like water filter replacements.

Per share, VIOT earned $0.24 per ADS. In terms of non-GAAP, net income was slightly higher at RMB127.6M, or $17.8M, which translates to a net income of $0.26 per ADS. These latest results are very bullish, but it is probably worth mentioning that VIOT, or the H1 FY2025 results to be more exact, benefited from government subsidies in China, which are meant to lower the purchase price of certain consumer products, but which may also not continue indefinitely. This could result in a slower pace of growth compared to the very strong results in H1 FY2025.

Even so, there is reason for optimism since growth is still likely to come in fast enough to justify valuations. GAAP EPADS totaled $0.38 per ADS TTM, which gives VIOT a P/E ratio of 6.1x with the stock at $2.32 per ADS. Such a low multiple has significant ramifications because it means VIOT does not have to grow all that fast before it can be deemed undervalued relative to growth.

For instance, according to the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch, if VIOT can grow earnings at a CAGR faster than 6.1% in the next 3-5 years, VIOT can be considered undervalued relative to growth. VIOT needs to grow earnings to just $0.46 per ADS, an increase of $0.08 with a base of $0.38, or better, three years from now, which looks very doable. Earnings have actually grown significantly faster than that in the past three years, with a CAGR of 25.6%.

Why there is reason for optimism about VIOT’s growth prospects

Keep in mind that just because earnings grew at a CAGR of 25+% in the past three years does not necessarily ensure earnings will grow just as fast in the next three years. Still, there is reason to be confident that VIOT can grow earnings at a CAGR of at least 6.1%, needed to warrant present valuations.

This is because the market for clean water is growing worldwide, especially in the Asia-Pacific region, because people are increasingly becoming aware of the importance of clean water to overall health. For instance, a recent report projects the market for water treatment will grow at a CAGR of 7.17% in 2025-2035 to $632.9B.

This desire for water, free of impurities as much as possible, is reflected in the growing consumption of, for example, bottled water by people around the world, which has become increasingly common, unlike the past when drinking bottled water was rare to see. VIOT is basically tapping into this structural shift in people’s preferences by offering an alternative to buying bottled water with solutions that provide people with clean water in their own homes.

While VIOT only covers a portion of the clean water market with its focus on offering home water solutions, specifically water purification products and related consumables, the market segment it focuses on has the potential to grow even faster than the wider market. VIOT itself has stated the market it serves is growing at about 8%.

VIOT would only need to grow sales in line with the growth of the overall market to be in a position to grow earnings at a rate that justifies long VIOT. If it also targets geographic regions that are growing faster, something like a CAGR of 10% for earnings growth in the next 3-5 years is a target that can be realistically achieved. This implies a price target of $3.80 per ADS.

Investor takeaways

It’s fair to say that VIOT has struggled in recent months with the stock in decline, which does not make it easy to argue in favor of long VIOT. VIOT is still up for the year, but it has seen its market cap sliced by close to half in the span of four months or so, which may serve to discourage potential buyers from stepping in. However, there is nonetheless a case to be made that now is a good time to step in.

The flip side of the large decline in the stock price is that the price to step in has come down. In fact, VIOT is arguably undervalued based on both the income statement and the balance sheet, as covered in greater detail earlier. VIOT is now close to being valued at the net cash on the books, and any further decline in the stock would only increase the disparity between what VIOT is valued at and what VIOT has in cash, or what can be quickly converted to cash. This could limit a much further decline in the stock price.

The most recent earnings results, which saw the top and the bottom line grow by 76.6% and 64.7%, respectively, in H1 FY2025, may not be representative of what to expect from VIOT in the long run, but VIOT has a good shot at growing earnings at a CAGR of 10% in the next 3-5 years to make long VIOT worth pursuing at current levels. This growth is backed by worldwide trends, with people increasingly attaching greater importance to clean drinking water and the impact that can have on an individual’s health.

The charts also suggest that the stock has reached a point where long VIOT makes sense. This is because there is reason to believe the reduction in the stock price has brought it close to a region where support is likely to be present. Getting in just above support makes for a good entry point, because of the likelihood of VIOT experiencing a reversal with the stock going from moving lower to moving higher.

I am bullish on VIOT with everything above in mind. The decline in the price of the stock is nothing to be pleased about, but it can also be viewed as an opportunity to get in on a stock that has a lot going for it at a much reduced price. The stock has gone down, but with growth prospects, charts, and valuations where they are, it will not take long for that to change.
